retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Glucagon -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: In addition to promoting glycogenolysis, glucagon also inhibits glycogenesis in the liver at the same time. Glycogen synthase is a significant regulatory enzyme in the glycogenesis pathway. This enzyme catalyzes the transfer of a glucosyl residue from UDP-glucose to a nonreducing end of the branched glycogen molecule. Glucagon induces phosphorylation of glycogen synthase and thereby inhibits glycogen synthase activity in the liver. Phosphorylation of glycogen synthase by multiple kinases, including PKA and other serine/threonine kinases leading to a graded inactivation, which leads to decreased glycogen synthesis and therefore increases the glucose output to the blood. [8] [23] Glucagon-induced phosphoenolpyruvate carboxykinase (PEPCK) is the enzyme in a rate-limiting step in hepatic gluconeogenesis. PKA activation leads to the activation of CREB, leading to the increased synthesis of PGC-1 protein. Both PGC-1 and HNF-4 are coactivators that increase the transcription of the PEPCK gene. In addition to the above functions, glucagon inhibits glycolysis by inhibiting phosphofructokinase-1. Glucagon also stimulates lipolysis and ketogenesis, and the ratio of glucagon to insulin perceived by the liver is a critical signal for the liver to convert fatty acids to ketone bodies as in diabetic hyperglycemic coma. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Gluconeogenesis -- Cellular Level -- Gluconeogenesis Reactions And Enzymes
retrieved_docs.content: In the cytosol, PEPCK decarboxylates oxaloacetate, which then rearranges to form phosphoenol pyruvate (PEP). PEPCK requires GTP and magnesium ions for activation. This transformation is the second step that reverses the nonequilibrium reaction catalyzed by pyruvate kinase.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Aerobic Glycolysis -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Hormonal control plays an important role in the regulation of glycolysis. Carbohydrate consumption and its breakdown lead to an increase in the levels of glucose and trigger the release of insulin, resulting in an increase in the ratio of insulin to glucagon. Insulin activates glucokinase, PFK1, and pyruvate kinase, the three important enzymes catalyzing the irreversible steps in glycolysis in order to process the available substrate. At the same time, low glucagon levels ensure that gluconeogenesis is inhibited. Long-term control through gene transcription is particularly important in fasting and starvation states and in diabetes when the ratio of insulin to glucagon is low. In such conditions, the synthesis of glucokinase, PFK1, and pyruvate kinase are decreased by modulation of gene transcription [1] [3] [1] .

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Gluconeogenesis -- Fundamentals -- Regulation of Gluconeogenesis
retrieved_docs.content: Multiple factors contribute to the regulation of substrates, enzymes, and reactions involved in gluconeogenesis, including: Glucagon regulates gluconeogenesis through [10] Changes in allosteric regulators (reduces the levels of fructose-2,6 bisphosphate) Covalent modification of enzyme activity (phosphorylation of pyruvate kinase results in its inactivation) Induction of enzymes gene expression (glucagon via CRE response elements increases the expression of PEPCK Acetyl CoA activates pyruvate carboxylase allosterically [11] Substrate availability might increase or decrease the rate of gluconeogenesis AMP inhibits fructose-1,6 bisphosphatase allosterically [12]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 4 -- Community 2773
retrieved_docs.content: Erythropetin and glukagon are both members of the broad class “Amino Acid, Peptide, or Protein.” As peptide hormones, they share the structural characteristic of being protein‑based signaling molecules, yet they serve very different physiological functions. Erythropetin regulates erythropoiesis by stimulating the production of red blood cells, while glukagon raises blood glucose levels by promoting glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis. Recognizing that these distinct hormones belong to the same molecular category highlights their common biochemical nature and informs both basic understanding of hormone biology and the development of protein‑based therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1603
retrieved_docs.content: The endokrinní systém (endocrine system) is the physiological network that regulates numerous metabolic processes and includes conditions such as متلازمة الأيض (metabolic syndrome) as a manifestation of its dysregulation. Within this system, pankreatické hormony (pancreatic hormones) represent a specific group of secretions that contribute to glucose homeostasis and other endocrine functions. Medical interventions that affect hormonal balance, such as kastrace (castration), are procedures that target the endokrinní systém, reflecting its role as a therapeutic focus in certain clinical contexts. Glycogenolytic hormones, which promote the breakdown of glycogen to glucose, constitute a distinct class of hormones that are not classified as pankreatické hormony, underscoring the diversity of hormonal actions within and beyond the endocrine system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1977
retrieved_docs.content: Klofibrát produces a physiologic effect of decreased lipolysis, thereby influencing lipid metabolism, but it is contraindicated in patients with تَشَمُّع كَبِدي (hepatic steatosis). Consequently, clinicians should consider the lipid‑lowering benefit of Klofibrát while avoiding its use in individuals with hepatic steatosis to prevent potential liver‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2884
retrieved_docs.content: Glycogenolytic hormones are a class of hormones that stimulate the breakdown of glycogen into glucose, thereby increasing blood glucose levels. Pancreatic hormones, which are secreted by the pancreas, fall within this category because they include glucagon, a key glycogenolytic hormone that promotes glycogenolysis. This relationship highlights the role of pancreatic endocrine function in regulating glucose metabolism through glycogenolytic activity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2404
retrieved_docs.content: Metabolic disorder (اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي) is a broad category of disease that includes carbohydrate metabolism disorder (اضطراب استقلاب الكربوهيدرات) as one of its subtypes. Within the hierarchy of carbohydrate metabolism disorder, other endocrine disorders are classified as a further subset, indicating that these endocrine conditions are considered specific forms of carbohydrate metabolism disorders. This hierarchical relationship highlights how metabolic disorders encompass a range of more specialized conditions, including those affecting carbohydrate processing and related endocrine functions.

Collectively, these hierarchical classifications link specific compounds to their mechanisms, chemical families, and clinical applications, providing a structured framework for endocrine evaluation, diagnostic testing, and comprehensive management of diabetes and related metabolic disorders, with biguanides explicitly identified as a subclass of non‑insulin blood‑glucose‑lowering agents used to reduce blood glucose in patients with diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 1267
retrieved_docs.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a broad category that includes several specific pancreatic endocrine disorders. Among its children are ارتفاع السكر في الدم, which describes the elevated blood‑glucose levels that characterize hyperglycemia; مرض السكري دون مضاعفات, which refers to diabetes that has not yet produced secondary complications; فرط أنسولين الدم, indicating an excess of circulating insulin often seen in early insulin‑resistance states or insulin‑producing tumors; and ورم غلوكاغوني, a rare glucagon‑secreting tumor (glucagonoma) that can also cause hyperglycemia. These conditions are interrelated through their impact on pancreatic hormone production and regulation of blood glucose, highlighting the importance of comprehensive assessment and management of pancreatic endocrine function in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1892
retrieved_docs.content: Endocrine system incision is identified as a direct procedural site within the endocrine system (endokrinní systém). The endocrine system (endokrinní systém) contains pancreatic hormones (pankreatické hormony), which are a component of the broader endocrine network. Castration is described as a procedure that targets the endocrine system, as reflected by the association with procedure_has_target_anatomy. Glycogenolytic hormones represent a larger hormonal class that includes pancreatic hormones, indicating that pancreatic hormones such as glucagon possess glycogenolytic activity. Together, these relationships illustrate how surgical interventions, hormonal subsets, and metabolic functions are interconnected within the endocrine system, informing both anatomical understanding and clinical considerations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2267
retrieved_docs.content: Topiramat is classified as a hypoglykemika, meaning it belongs to the class of agents that lower blood glucose levels. Recognizing topiramat as a hypoglycemic medication is important for understanding its potential impact on glucose metabolism and for guiding appropriate patient monitoring and care.

Metabolic disorders of lipoproteins and other lipidemias are a subcategory of disorders of lipid metabolism, inversely linked (ISA) to the Arabic term اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي (metabolic disorder) and classified as اضطراب استقلاب الدهون (disorder of fat metabolism). This group maps to type IIa hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ) and includes early‑onset diabetes/hyperglycemia (ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد), demonstrating clinical overlap between dyslipidemia and glucose‑regulation disorders. Autosomal dominant inheritance is the pattern for type IIa hyperlipoproteinemia, and the lipid‑modifying drug class fibrates (e.g., bezafibrát, clofibrate, fenofibrate) is used to treat these disorders; clofibrate is contraindicated in liver cirrhosis. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 1 -- Community 223
retrieved_docs.content: Insulin aspart, a human insulin analog, exerts its pharmacologic action by targeting the human insulin receptor (INSR protein) and produces a physiologic effect of decreased lipolysis. Klofibrát, a fibrate‑type lipid‑lowering agent, also leads to decreased lipolysis as a physiologic effect. Thus, both insulin aspart and klofibrát share the outcome of reduced lipolysis, although insulin aspart achieves this through direct activation of the INSR protein, while klofibrát acts via its own distinct metabolic pathway. Recognizing this common effect can inform therapeutic decisions when managing conditions related to lipid metabolism and glucose regulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 113
retrieved_docs.content: of the endocrine system and an endocrine system incision are procedures whose direct target is the endocrine system (endokrinní systém). The endocrine system encompasses the thyroid gland as one of its child structures, and it is closely linked to pancreatic hormones (pankreatické hormony), which represent a major component of the overall endocrine network. In this context, pancreatic hormones are related to glycogenolytic hormones, indicating that glycogenolytic activity is part of the hormonal functions mediated by the pancreas. The thyroid gland is also noted as a site where findings of pituitary insufficiency (قصور الدَّرَقِيَّة) may be observed, reflecting the interdependence of the pituitary and thyroid axes. Additionally, castration procedures are recorded as having the endocrine system as their anatomical target, underscoring the system’s role in hormonal regulation beyond the thyroid alone. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2075
retrieved_docs.content: Glibenklamid and glimepiride are both members of the sulfonylurea class of oral agents used for blood glucose lowering. As sulfonylureas, they act by stimulating pancreatic insulin secretion, thereby helping to control hyperglycemia in patients with diabetes mellitus. Recognizing that glibenklamid and glimepiride belong to the same therapeutic group informs clinicians about their similar mechanisms of action, potential side effect profiles, and considerations for interchangeable use in diabetes management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2467
retrieved_docs.content: Clofibrate (klofrolát) exerts a physiologic effect by decreasing lipolysis, which contributes to its lipid‑lowering action. Because of its pharmacologic profile, clofibrate is contraindicated in patients with renal failure (قصور الكلى), and should be avoided in this population to prevent adverse outcomes.
